speaker: interviewer
text: This is the interview with Dr. Helen Fagin. Today is February 11, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: The card on the right is my mother's personal card. This is her maiden name, Ewa Heiman. This obviously, is her card from the time before her marriage to my father. The card on the left is my father's business card. It says S. Neumark, Radomsko, Rynek 9, which was the name of the square where we resided and where he had his business. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I began teaching courses on the Holocaust at the University of Miami, where I was Associate Professor of English. The first course I offered was Literature of the Holocaust in 1970, and I began sensitizing the entire community to the necessity of using the Holocaust to teach the moral lessons of the Holocaust. I forced the community to confront the Holocaust, and finally, we arrived at the point where we had our first Yom HaShoah. And this plaque was presented to me at the Yom HaShoah by the organizations, Jewish organizations, in the city of Miami. And you have asked me to read it to you, so I shall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'In tribute and recognition to Helen Fagin for her leadership and inspiration in keeping alive in South Florida the memory of the martyred six million Jews who died in such horror during the Holocaust at the hands of the Nazis and-- and their anti-Semitic cohorts throughout Europe during World War II. For almost simple-handedly-- I'm sorry-- for almost single-handedly forcing the community to confront the Holocaust and its meaning as a unique Jewish experience that has enormous-- enormous consequences for all humanity fulfilling Santayana's concept that those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it. For her invaluable influence as an educator on countless numbers of students of all ages in our institutions of higher learning and in seminars and encounters throughout the general community, for her love and devotion to the Jewish people. Signed, Central Agency for Jewish Education, Community Relations Committee of the Greater Miami Jewish Federation, Rabbinical Association of Greater Miami, Zachor Institute for Holocaust Studies. Presented at the Greater Miami Annual Community Holocaust Memorial observance on Yom HaShoah April 10, 1983. Quotation from Edmund Burke-- 'The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing.'' [INAUDIBLE] Having begun to teach literature of the Holocaust and other related courses at the University of Miami, it occurred to me that the most important mission that I could fulfill is to use the Holocaust to teach moral lessons, constructive moral lessons of the Holocaust to my students. I avoided throughout my career any references to my own suffering and to my own experiences, since I never wished to elicit any sympathy from anybody for me, personally. However, the Holocaust as an experience, the Holocaust as dehumanization of another human being, the Holocaust as amoral behavior, and the Holocaust as a behavior by civilized people of totally negating the humanity of other people was to me a very important lesson that I must convey to my students. And most of all, I had to convey to them that to be a human being means not to dehumanize another human being. My favorite quotation is from Joshua Abraham Heschel, who wrote in his book, When he tried to find the definition for man, he then said, 'man is simply a human being,' and that is the definition. The definition of man resides in these two words in being human. And any man who denies humanity of another human being dehumanizes that other human being and does not deserve the name of man. This has been my commitment. This has been my mission, and indeed, I have been known to use that, rather than personal experiences, and it worked on all audiences-- on my students and adult audiences. I have done that when I was training teachers for the United States Holocaust Memorial Council and Museum, where for the last five years, I have been serving as a chairman of the Education Committee for the museum. Now I am a member of this committee, as well as a member of the academic committee for the museum. This also was the reason why I decided to build a Holocaust memorial in Miami Beach. I decided to do it as a monument and a memorial to my parents, but I decided for it to have to have an educational component, and that memorial, indeed, is a very powerful educational statement. I wrote the history of the Holocaust on the black granite of the memorial. I used pictures, and I wrote the captions, which actually informed, if you were, about the human experience that took place in the Holocaust. In November of 1994, I was very honored by a presidential appointment by President Bill Clinton, who appointed me to the Presidential Advisory Board for the purpose of building the World War II Memorial in Washington, DC. And indeed, we started work immediately. We already vouchsafed a site, a very prestigious site on the mall, and right now, we are discussing guidelines for design. I was asked to serve as the chairperson of the subcommittee, of the advice design subcommittee of the advisory board to the American Battle Monuments Commission. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is the certificate of appointment. This photograph was taken in the White House on November 11, 1994 at the breakfast given by the President for those serving on the advisory board in American Battle Monument Commission in honor of the dedication of the site for the World War II Memorial, which is going to receive, hopefully, one of the nicest monuments ever built on the mall in Washington, DC.
